references.title: Logs or Self-Reports? Misalignment Between Behavioral Trace Data and Surveys When Modeling Learner Achievement Goal Orientation

extracted.Courses: 2 editions of a Data Science course from University of Michigan
extracted.Features: [mastery_approach, mastery_avoicdance, performance_approach, performance_avoidance, performance_controlling, performance_autonomous, emailN_count, notebookNcount, bonusN_sharing, extraN_submitted, extraN_count, additionalN_count]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
SAmple size was reduced to 191 students because not all students had answered all quizzes. 

Analysis starts with CFA to check fit of answers with Goal complex Theory. 

Continuous variables in Trace data were log-transformed. 

Survey and Log datasets are treated separately since one the research questions is the comparison between them. 

Clustering is used on both datasets separately (Weeks 1-2) and for weeks (3-4) the clustering is done separately and with the combined datasetw.
extracted.Groups Created:
Weeks 1-2 on Trace data: 3

Weeks 3-4 on Trace data: 3
extracted.Cluster Description:
Weeks 1-2: 

Less Engaged (95): Rarely interact with optional course materials  

Mastery and Performance (52): generated highest counts of log data with assignments and extra assignments 

Performance (44): Higher interest in materials bearing influence on their grade. 

Weeks 3-4: 

Performance and weak mastery (43) 

Less Engaged (135)

MAstery and Performance Controlling (13)
extracted.Implications:
A refined definition of what achievement goals are:

Expectations, followed by Action, followed by remembering what the goals are.

Discrepancies between trace and survey could be used to identify students struggling with SRL skills.
extracted.Comments:
CFA shows that trace data fits better with Goal Complex Theory than the Survey constructs. 

BAsed on these results, most of the discussion is centered around the Trace data clusters. 

Moreover, the authors find that there is a poor mapping of the survey data to the actual behavioral data → students tend to overestimate their intents.
extracted.Limitations:
Features need to be revised to ensure that they capture different motivations. 

Does not consider any variable beyond instrumentation within the course.

annotations.text: Learning analytics is being constantly integrated in learning environments. However, most of the markers for student success derive from self-reported measures. Study compares the effectiveness of logs vs self-reports as effective representations of achievement goals.

The authors create goal clusters using latent variable mixture modelling and the results show that there is a misalignement between both sources of data. More specifically, self-repoorts before learning do not translate into learning relevant behaviour measurable by learning logs. More specifically, self-reports seem to be overly optimistic measures of student motivation and engagement.
